perturbation.original_text: Payment IBM invoices will specify the amount due. Payment is due and payable by month end for any invoice received by the 10th of the month, otherwise payment is due thirty (30) days from receipt of invoice. Customer agrees to pay accordingly, including any late payment fees. Payment will be made in United States dollars.
perturbation.changed_text: Payment IBM invoices will specify the amount due. Payment is due and payable sixty (60) days from receipt of invoice. Customer agrees to pay accordingly, including any late payment fees. Payment will be made in United States dollars.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the payment terms to 60 days effectively removes the customer's right to dispute charges within a reasonable timeframe, increasing the risk of non-compliance. While payment timelines are generally contractual, drastically extending them without explicit waiver of dispute rights introduces legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-607. Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach After Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over.
perturbation.law_explanation: By extending the payment due date to 60 days without addressing dispute resolution, the modified text implicitly shortens the 'reasonable time' a buyer has under UCC § 2-607 to notify the seller of a breach *after* accepting goods/services, creating a legal contradiction as it unduly restricts buyer's rights.

perturbation.justification: The modification extends the payment due date to 60 days. The Law Explanation states this implicitly shortens the buyer's 'reasonable time' under UCC § 2-607 to notify the seller of a breach after accepting goods/services. The Perturbation Explanation claims that this modification effectively removes the customer's right to dispute charges within a reasonable timeframe, increasing the risk of non-compliance. Since the change unduly restricts the buyer's right to dispute charges, a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted, making the contract non-compliant. This aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Waiver of Jury Trial The parties waive any right to a jury trial in any proceeding arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may consider waiving their right to a jury trial in any proceeding arising out of or related to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: While generally enforceable, jury trial waivers require knowing and voluntary consent. This change weakens the waiver language, creating potential for a court to find it unenforceable, particularly if there's an imbalance of power or lack of informed consent. This omission creates a legal risk by making the waiver's validity uncertain.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 7th Amendment to the United States Constitution.
perturbation.law_explanation: The 7th Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While this right can be waived, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. The modified language's suggestion that parties "may consider" waiving this right creates ambiguity and potentially undermines the validity of the waiver, as it doesn't unequivocally demonstrate a clear and intentional relinquishment of this constitutional right.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the waiver of jury trial, creating a legal contradiction. The original text stated that "The parties waive any right to a jury trial", which is a clear and direct waiver. The changed text states "The parties may consider waiving their right to a jury trial", which introduces ambiguity. The Seventh Amendment, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, ensures the right to a jury trial unless waived. The law explanation correctly states that the modified text undermines the validity of the waiver since it does not unequivocally demonstrate a clear and intentional relinquishment of this constitutional right. This falls under the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction because it removes essential information regarding the waiver, making it non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: Actions Period Neither party will bring a legal action related to this Agreement more than two years after the cause of action accrued.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither party will bring a legal action related to this Agreement more than one year after the cause of action accrued.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the limitations period to one year could violate state statutes of limitations for certain contract claims, making the clause unenforceable and creating legal risk. The original 2 year limitation period is shortened to 1 year, which could be shorter than the legally prescribed period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules § 213
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 213 prescribes a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract actions. By reducing the contractual limitations period to one year, the modified text directly contradicts this statutory provision. While parties can agree to shorten the limitations period, it must be 'reasonable'. A one-year period may be deemed unreasonable, especially in complex commercial transactions, and therefore unenforceable, thus contradicting the law.

Civil Practice Law & Rules (CVP) CHAPTER 8, ARTICLE 2 § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property;

Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws CVP - Civil Practice Law and Rules Article 2 - Limitations of Time 213 - Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract; on Sealed Instrument; on Bond or Note, and Mortgage Upon Real Universal Citation: NY CPLR § 213 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 213. Actions to be commenced within six years: where not otherwise provided for; on contract; on sealed instrument; on bond or note, and mortgage upon real property; by state based on misappropriation of public property; based on mistake; by corporation against director, officer or stockholder; based on fraud. The following actions must be commenced within six years: 1. an action for which no limitation is specifically prescribed by law; 2. an action upon a contractual obligation or liability, express or implied, except as provided in section two hundred thirteen-a or two hundred fourteen-i of this article or article 2 of the uniform commercial code or article 36-B of the general business law; 3. an action upon a sealed instrument; 4. an action upon a bond or note, the payment of which is secured by a mortgage upon real property, or upon a bond or note and mortgage so secured, or upon a mortgage of real property, or any interest therein; (a) In any action on an instrument described under this subdivision, if the statute of limitations is raised as a defense, and if that defense is based on a claim that the instrument at issue was accelerated
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the limitations period for legal action from two years to one year, which contradicts NY CPLR § 213, which prescribes a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract actions. Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states this six-year limit: "Actions to Be Commenced Within Six Years: Where Not Otherwise Provided For; on Contract". The Perturbation Explanation also notes this reduction could violate state statutes, creating legal risk, and the Law Explanation supports this by stating the reduced period may be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable.
